reason: Both outputs satisfy all five required visual-evidence items (sun + 6 planets, distinct orbit distances, readable labels, visible bloom, whole-system framing) and both compiled, ran, and did not time out (agentTimedOut=false, exit 0). Three.js is visually somewhat stronger: CSS2D labels are attached to planet meshes so they track correctly, orbits are clear concentric rings, planets actually revolve via pivot groups, and the sun has bloom plus an additive halo/corona and a ring planet. Aura3D's render is attractive (soft sun bloom, clearcoat planets, translucent orbit disks) but lower production quality on two points visible in the screenshot vs source: planets only spin in place rather than orbit (despite labeled 'speed'), and labels are static HTML overlays positioned by a hand-rolled cos/sin percentage projection that does not follow the orbit camera and sit offset from their planets. Conversely, Aura's source is materially more modular and concise (185 vs 279 lines) via its declarative scene/effects/camera builder API. The visual edge for Three.js and the modularity edge for Aura3D roughly cancel, so neither is materially better overall. Prompt 03 is not a designated hard prompt (07/08/10), so hardPromptPass is false.
